summary: A brief transitional passage, then the Gemara continues: Rabbi Yochanan rules that a non-priest who sets up the wood arrangement on the altar (sidur etzim) is liable for the death penalty. Rabbi Zeira objects: is there any Temple service valid when performed by a non-priest but also a death-penalty offense? The discussion refines Rabbi Yochanan's ruling and establishes the precise conditions under which wood-arrangement is considered a complete service requiring priestly status.

glossary.en: Liable to death penalty; a non-priest who performs certain Temple services is liable to death at the hand of Heaven.
Complete service; a Temple task that is a full, standalone act of divine service requiring priesthood.

rashiTranslations.en: Brief transitional text connecting the previous discussion.
R. Asi citing R. Yochanan: non-priest who sets up wood arrangement is liable to death.
R. Zeira's objection: is there any service valid when performed by a non-priest that also incurs the death penalty?
Wonder: is there no service valid when done by a non-priest but punishable?
Ash removal at night is valid but done without priestly garments - different category.
Revision of R. Yochanan's ruling: the service is valid when done by a non-priest, but the non-priest has transgressed.
Another difficulty: daytime service by non-priest - is there a daytime service that is valid but incurs the penalty?
Further difficulty: complete service by non-priest - is there such a case where it's both complete and punishable?
Mar Zutra asks: what exactly does R. Yochanan's ruling cover?
Response: the mishna mentions slaughter because that is the natural starting point, not because slaughter is the subject of the dispute.
Alternative version of R. Zeira's objection about non-priest wood arrangement.
It wrote here 'they shall place fire' concerning the morning wood arrangement.
Death penalty applies; 'dismantle it' - a non-kohen need not dismantle it.
A non-kohen can dismantle it without violating anything.
But limbs and fat - the burning of them lasts all night.
We categorized them above (24a) as one of the four services.
That a non-kohen is liable to death for performing.
And the removal of ash - written with kohen requirement.
It is a difficulty. Is there really a service valid only by night but invalid for a non-kohen?
Rather it is a daytime service.
As written 'and the kohen shall kindle wood on it in the morning, in the morning.'
'In the morning' indicates it is a daytime service.
If so - since it is so significant as a daytime service.
And a non-kohen is liable for it.
It implies a daytime service requires a lottery.
Since Rava objected 'if so it requires a lottery.'
Implying this service should require one.
But we do not teach that the wood arrangement requires a lottery.
Since it is not a daytime service.
If the time of slaughter arrives - if dawn has broken.
Time to slaughter the tamid, since slaughter is invalid at night.
As written (Lev. 19) 'on the day of your sacrifice.'
There is no remedy - if it was slaughtered at night.
There is a remedy - if the logs were arranged at night.
They can be dismantled and re-arranged by day.
Therefore the Sages did not trouble the men to go up to the roof.
And check - since once was enough.
One time moonlight caused them to think dawn broke.
And they slaughtered the tamid as recounted in chapter three.
But if wood was arranged by moonlight there is a remedy.
Since the arrangement stays on the Temple floor until dawn; if they erred with moonlight it matters not.
Since there is a remedy.
An avodah follows it - the wood arrangement is followed by the limb arrangement.
It is invalid for a non-kohen making one liable to death.

display.hint: Connects the previous analysis to R. Yochanan's new ruling.

learning.summary: A brief transitional passage completing the previous discussion before R. Yochanan's new statement.
learning.ahaMoment: The Gemara signals a new topic is beginning.

argumentFlow.label: Transition
argumentFlow.text: Brief passage connecting the Rav Hamnuna difficulty to R. Yochanan's new ruling.

misconceptions: This is a major independent passage - it is only a transitional connector.

quizSeeds.q: What is the brief passage at the start of 27b?
quizSeeds.a: A transitional connector from the previous discussion about non-priest services to R. Yochanan's new ruling.

display.whats: R. Yochanan rules that a non-priest who sets up the wood arrangement on the altar incurs the death penalty. R. Zeira objects: every service incurring the death penalty must also be invalid when done by a non-priest. R. Yochanan's ruling is revised multiple times to account for these objections.
display.hint: The criterion is whether wood-arrangement is a 'complete service' (avodah temimah). If it is, a non-priest doing it both transgresses and renders the service invalid.

learning.summary: R. Asi cites R. Yochanan: a non-priest who arranges the wood on the altar is liable to death. R. Zeira objects: is there any Temple service that is valid when performed by a non-priest but also punishable by death? The two categories seem mutually exclusive: either the act is 'complete service' (in which case a non-priest incurs death AND invalidates it) or it is not complete service (in which case a non-priest may do it validly and without penalty). Wood-arrangement that is not a complete, standalone service falls outside both categories. The Gemara refines R. Yochanan's ruling through multiple rounds of objection.
learning.ahaMoment: The categories 'liable to death' and 'valid when done by non-priest' are mutually exclusive - a service is either complete enough to require priestly status (and thus a non-priest is both punished and the service invalid) or not complete enough (and a non-priest is neither punished nor does he invalidate it). Wood-arrangement sits in an unusual category.
learning.memoryAnchor: Death penalty + invalid service always go together. Valid but punishable seems to be a contradiction - unless the service is special.
learning.openQuestion: Is there any Temple service that a non-priest performs validly (the offering is not disqualified) but for which he is still punished?

argumentFlow.label: R. Yochanan: non-priest wood arranger = death
argumentFlow.text: R. Asi citing R. Yochanan: a non-priest who sets up the wood arrangement on the altar is liable to death.

argumentFlow.label: R. Zeira: valid non-priest service cannot also be punishable
argumentFlow.text: R. Zeira: is there any service that is valid when performed by a non-priest but also punishable? These categories seem contradictory.

argumentFlow.label: Is there no such case?
argumentFlow.text: The Gemara wonders: is there truly no Temple service that is valid by a non-priest but still punishable?

argumentFlow.label: Revised ruling: non-priest transgresses but service valid
argumentFlow.text: Revision of R. Yochanan's position: the wood arrangement is valid even if done by a non-priest (the service is not disqualified), but the non-priest has transgressed.

argumentFlow.label: Further difficulty: daytime + complete service
argumentFlow.text: Additional objections: the ruling must specify that this is a daytime service AND a complete service before the death penalty applies.

argumentFlow.label: Mar Zutra's query
argumentFlow.text: Mar Zutra asks what exactly R. Yochanan's ruling covers, given all the objections and revisions.

halachic: death penalty for non-priest Temple service
theological: Temple service as exclusively priestly domain
narrative: multiple rounds of revision to R. Yochanan's ruling

requiresUnderstanding: death penalty for priestly service violation
misconceptions: Any service a non-priest performs validly he can also do without penalty - R. Zeira shows that valid-but-punishable seems impossible, but the case of wood arrangement might be exceptional.
Wood arrangement by a non-priest automatically invalidates the altar service - R. Yochanan's revised ruling says the service may still be valid even if the non-priest transgressed.

visualizableElements.label: Valid vs punishable service categories
visualizableElements.description: Three categories: (1) complete priestly service - non-priest is punished AND service is invalid; (2) non-priestly service - non-priest may do it validly; (3) the problematic middle category

visualizableElements.label: Non-priest at the altar fire
visualizableElements.description: A non-kohen placing logs on the altar fire - does this transgress and/or invalidate the service?

quizSeeds.q: What paradox does R. Zeira identify in R. Yochanan's ruling about non-priests arranging wood?
quizSeeds.a: If a service is valid when performed by a non-priest, it should not be punishable; if it is punishable, the service should be invalid. 'Valid but punishable' seems contradictory.

quizSeeds.q: What is the revised understanding of R. Yochanan's ruling about wood arrangement?
quizSeeds.a: The wood arrangement remains valid even if performed by a non-priest, but the non-priest has transgressed a prohibition - he is punishable but the service is not disqualified.

sugyot.finalRuling: Non-priest wood arrangement: the ruling and its precise conditions are debated and revised across 27b-28a. The service may be valid while the non-priest transgresses.
